content.problemStatement:
Gormint aunty is very upset with the government and Imran wants to prove that his government is not sold out. Gormint aunty gives an ethical problem which only an honest person could solve. Help Imran solve this question.

You are given two integers NNN and KKK. Find the minimum number of colors needed to color a **circular array** of size NNN such that the following condition is satisfied:


For any subarray of size KKK, there should not be any repeating colors.
Also construct one such array. If there are many possible arrays, you may print **any one** of them. Each color will be represented by a unique number between 111 and XXX, where XXX is the number of distinct colors needed to build this array.

**Note:** A circular array is one wherein the first element is adjacent to the last. For example, given the circular array [1,2,3][1,2,3][1,2,3], all possible subarrays of size 2 are: [1,2],[2,3],[3,1][1,2], [2,3], [3,1][1,2],[2,3],[3,1].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK, as described in the statement.


Output Format
For each test case, output two lines:



The first line should contain a single integer XXX, denoting the minimum number of colors needed to build the array.
The second line should contain NNN space-separated integers denoting the elements of the array. Each element of the array should be an integer between 111 and XXX, denoting the color of this element; and no subarray of this array of length KKK should contain repeated colors. If there are multiple such arrays, **print any of them**.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: All elements of the given array are distinct, so it obviously satisfies the condition.
It can be shown that in this case, 444 colors is not enough to construct a solution.
Test case 222: Every subarray of length 111 contains a single element, and hence no repeated colors.
